grievance_description.brief_description_of_grievance:
I am filing this grievance because I felt that my medical and personal care needs were not handled appropriately during my visit to the Senior Care Partners P.A.C.E. center on 2025-11-28, and the way the situation was communicated to me left me feeling dismissed and unsafe.

On Friday, November 28, 2025, I arrived at the center around 8:30 a.m. for my scheduled primary care and physical therapy appointments. I use a rollator walker and have a history of congestive heart failure, diabetes, and arthritis in both knees. I had been told earlier in the week by my nurse, Angela, that my morning appointment would include a medication review with the doctor because I had been experiencing dizziness and two near-falls at home. I was specifically worried about my blood pressure pills and the new pain medication that was added last month.

During my time at the center that day, several things happened that I believe did not meet the standard of care that I have come to expect:

1. **Medication review did not occur as promised:**
   - I waited in the exam room for over 45 minutes past my scheduled appointment time. When the provider (a covering nurse practitioner whose name I believe was "Kelly") finally came in, she seemed rushed and said she only had time to check my blood pressure and refill my medications. I tried to explain that I had been having more dizziness, especially when getting up from a chair or using the bathroom at night, and that I had almost fallen twice in the last two weeks.
   - I specifically asked if we could go through my medication list one by one, because I sometimes get confused and I worry that something might be interacting badly. She glanced at my chart and said, "Everything looks fine, we’ll just keep you on the same regimen," without asking follow-up questions or explaining why she thought the medications were okay.
   - I left the exam room feeling like my concerns about dizziness and falls were brushed off. As an 82-year-old woman living alone, falls are a serious fear of mine, and I had made a point of asking for this review several times.

2. **Inadequate response to dizziness during physical therapy:**
   - After the rushed medical visit, I went to my scheduled physical therapy session. About 15 minutes into the exercises, while I was doing sit-to-stand repetitions from a low chair, I suddenly felt lightheaded, sweaty, and like the room was spinning. I told the physical therapist’s assistant (a younger woman whose name I did not catch) that I felt very dizzy and needed to stop.
   - She told me to "just push through a little more" because we "needed to meet our goals" for the day. I had to insist twice that I needed to sit and rest. When I stood up again to walk back toward my rollator, I became even more dizzy and had to grab the parallel bars to avoid falling. I felt extremely unsafe.
   - Only after I raised my voice and said, "I really don’t feel right; I feel like I might pass out," did she finally call the nurse. My blood pressure, when checked, was 92/58, which is much lower than my usual. They gave me water and had me sit for about 20 minutes, but no one came back to talk through whether my medications or therapy plan needed to be adjusted.

3. **Communication style felt dismissive and disrespectful:**
   - When I asked the nurse practitioner later in the hallway if my blood pressure medicine might be too strong, she said, "Low blood pressure is better than high at your age," in a joking tone. I did not find this funny and felt she was not taking my symptoms seriously.
   - I also overheard the physical therapy assistant telling another staff member that I was "being dramatic" and that I "always get worked up over nothing." Hearing that really hurt my feelings, especially because I try very hard to follow instructions and not be a burden.

4. **Impact on my sense of safety and trust in the program:**
   - I live alone at 1847 West Maple Street, Apartment 3B, in Battle Creek, and my daughter, Sandra, can only visit a few times a week because she works full-time and has children. I rely on the P.A.C.E. program to help me stay safe and independent at home.
   - After this incident, I went home feeling anxious and afraid to take my evening medications because I did not know if they were contributing to my dizziness. I ended up skipping my nighttime blood pressure pill, which I know is not ideal, but I was honestly scared of fainting in my bathroom or kitchen with no one around.
   - I also did not sleep well that night because I kept replaying the way I was spoken to and the feeling of almost fainting during therapy. I worried that if I fall and break a hip, I will end up in a nursing home, which is exactly what the P.A.C.E. program is supposed to help me avoid.

5. **What I am asking for with this grievance:**
   - I would like a thorough review of my medications, especially the blood pressure medicines and the new pain medication, to see if adjustments need to be made to prevent further dizziness.
   - I would like staff, particularly in therapy, to be reminded that when participants say they are dizzy or feel unsafe, they should stop the activity right away and check on us, not encourage us to "push through" when we are clearly in distress.
   - I would also appreciate an apology from the staff members involved for the way my concerns were minimized and for the comment that I was being "dramatic." I do not want anyone to get in trouble, but I do want the team to understand how frightening and humiliating this felt from my perspective.

I am filing this grievance not only for myself but also because I know there are other older participants who may be too shy or nervous to speak up. I want to feel confident that when I come to the center and say I am not feeling well, I will be listened to carefully and treated with respect, and that my symptoms will be taken seriously, especially when they involve dizziness and a risk of falling.

resolution.name_and_title_of_person_completing_form: Daniel R. McCarthy, MSW, Participant Services Coordinator
resolution.brief_description_of_resolution:
Grievance received on 2025-11-29 and acknowledged with a phone call to participant, Ms. Margaret Henderson, on 2025-12-01 at 10:30 a.m. A written acknowledgment letter was mailed to her home address the same day as requested.

1. **Immediate safety and clinical follow-up:**
   - On 2025-12-01, Ms. Henderson was scheduled for an urgent in-person visit with her primary care provider of record, Dr. Alicia Patel, at the P.A.C.E. center. During this visit, a comprehensive medication reconciliation was completed. Dr. Patel identified that Ms. Henderson was on two blood pressure medications (lisinopril and amlodipine) and a recently added nighttime dose of gabapentin for neuropathic pain.
   - Based on Ms. Henderson’s reported symptoms of dizziness and near-falls, as well as blood pressure readings documented in the chart (including the low reading of 92/58 on 2025-11-28), Dr. Patel reduced the dose of amlodipine and discontinued the nighttime gabapentin. A plan was established for home blood pressure monitoring three times per week by the home care nurse for the next four weeks.
   - The physical therapy plan of care was also reviewed the same day by the lead PT, Jennifer Collins, DPT. Standing exercises were temporarily modified to seated and supported activities only, with clear instructions that Ms. Henderson is not to perform sit-to-stands or ambulation tasks if she reports dizziness or lightheadedness. The PT team agreed to increase rest breaks and to reassess her tolerance weekly.

2. **Staff coaching and education:**
   - The nurse practitioner who saw Ms. Henderson on 2025-11-28 (identified as Kelly Johnson, NP) and the physical therapy assistant involved in the incident were each individually counseled by their supervisors.
   - The clinical director met with NP Johnson on 2025-12-02 to review expectations around medication review when a participant specifically reports new or worsening symptoms (in this case, dizziness and near-falls). NP Johnson acknowledged that she was running behind schedule that day and did not allocate enough time to address Ms. Henderson’s concerns thoroughly. She expressed regret for her comment about "low blood pressure being better than high" and agreed that the remark could be perceived as dismissive.
   - The rehabilitation manager met with the physical therapy assistant on 2025-12-03 to review appropriate responses when a participant reports dizziness or feeling unsafe during therapy. The assistant admitted she encouraged Ms. Henderson to continue the exercise longer than was appropriate and did not immediately stop the activity when dizziness was first reported. She stated she did not intend to be disrespectful, but she understood that her behavior could have placed the participant at risk. She denied calling Ms. Henderson "dramatic" but acknowledged that she may have spoken casually to another staff member in a way that could have been overheard and misinterpreted.
   - Both staff members were required to complete a refresher training on fall risk prevention, person-centered communication, and respectful language with participants. Completion of this training will be documented in their employee files.

3. **Program-wide quality improvement actions:**
   - This incident was discussed anonymously (without using Ms. Henderson’s name) at the 2025-12-05 Interdisciplinary Team (IDT) meeting as a quality improvement case study. The team reviewed protocols for:
     • Responding immediately to reports of dizziness during therapy or other activities.
     • Ensuring that medication concerns raised by participants trigger a focused review, even on busy clinic days.
     • Avoiding joking or minimizing language when discussing serious symptoms with older adults.
   - As a result, the team agreed to implement a simple checklist for therapy sessions that includes a standardized question at the beginning and midway through each session: "Are you feeling dizzy, lightheaded, short of breath, or unsafe right now?" If a participant answers yes, staff must stop the activity and document the response and action taken.

4. **Communication with participant and outcome:**
   - On 2025-12-06, the undersigned (Daniel McCarthy, MSW) and Dr. Patel met with Ms. Henderson in person at the center to review the findings of the grievance investigation and the steps taken. We apologized for the way her concerns were handled on 2025-11-28 and for the distress and fear she experienced.
   - We explained the changes made to her medication regimen, the modifications to her therapy plan, and the additional staff training and program-wide changes. Ms. Henderson stated that she appreciated being listened to and felt relieved that her dizziness was being taken seriously. She expressed that she felt more comfortable continuing in the program, provided that staff follow the new plan and communicate clearly with her when she reports symptoms.
   - Ms. Henderson confirmed that she would like oral updates if any further changes are made to her care plan and requested a copy of the updated medication list, which was printed and given to her at the end of the meeting.

5. **Service Delivery Request (SDR):**
   - An SDR was completed on 2025-12-01 to document the changes in Ms. Henderson’s medication regimen, the enhanced home blood pressure monitoring schedule, and the revised physical therapy plan of care focused on fall prevention and safety.

In summary, the grievance was **substantiated** with respect to inadequate attention to reported dizziness and communication that felt dismissive. Corrective actions have been implemented at both the individual staff level and the program level. Ms. Henderson has been informed of these actions and has indicated that she is satisfied with the plan moving forward, while we continue to monitor her closely for any further concerns.
